segments.s176-g41: من (I) is the grammatical subject placed before the verb for emphasis; آن here is a demonstrative ('that'/'then') pointing to the scene; دیدم is the past tense verb 'I saw.' از + proper name (از گیو) is a classical Persian construction meaning 'I saw Giv' (literally 'I saw of Giv'); کز is the relative particle 'who/that' introducing a relative clause; پیل و شیر are the coordinated nouns 'elephant and lion.'
segments.s176-g42: نبیند is the archaic/poetic negative form of 'دیدن' (here meaning 'to be fazed/see as a threat' → 'did not fear/was not daunted'); جهاندیده is a compound adjective 'world‑wise/experienced' modifying مرد; مرد دلیر is the noun phrase 'brave man/hero.' The sequence is a relative clause describing گیو introduced by کز in the previous segment.
segments.s176-g43: بر آن سان is an adverbial phrase 'in that manner/so much so'; کجا here is rhetorical ('wherever'/'whither') and pairs with بردمد (an older form meaning 'he took himself/advanced' or 'went'); روز جنگ is a time expression 'the day of battle' placed after the verb, typical of Persian word order.
segments.s176-g44: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' or 'by virtue of'; نفسش is 'his breath' (instrumental cause); به دریا indicates location 'in/into the sea'; بسوزد is a classical-form verb 'would burn/be scorched' (here used as a generalizing or hortatory present); نهنگ is the subject 'whale.' Together: 'a whale in the sea would burn from his breath.'
segments.s176-g45: نخست is an adverb 'first/at first'; اندر آمد is a compound verb 'came in/entered'; به before an instrument (به گرز گران) functions as 'with/by' in classical Persian, and گرز گران is 'heavy mace' with the adjective گران ('heavy') following the noun, a common adjective‑after‑noun placement in older Persian.
segments.s176-g46: همی is a durative particle marking continuous or repeated action ('kept on'); کوفت is the past tense 'struck' (here in the sense 'smote'/'beat'); چون introduces a simile 'like/as'; پتک آهنگران is a simile 'the mallet/sledge of blacksmiths' (آهنگران in the genitive), so 'he struck as a blacksmith's hammer would.'
segments.s176-g47: The repeated preposition به functions as an instrumental 'with/by' before a string of coordinated nouns: اسپ (steed), گرز (mace), پای (foot — 'on foot'), and رکیب (companion/associate). The coordination lists the modes or accoutrements in which/with which action takes place; the repeated به is a classical poetic style.
segments.s176-g48: سوار is the subject 'rider/horseman'; از فراز is a prepositional phrase 'from/on high/from the summit'; اندر آمد is 'came in/descended'; به شیب marks the destination 'to the slope' (literally 'to the incline'). The clause describes the rider's descent from the heights onto a slope.
segments.s176-g185: همه هدیه‌ها نزد شاه آورید is an imperative clause. The direct object (همه هدیه‌ها, 'all the gifts') appears before the prepositional phrase نزد شاه ('to/into the presence of the king') and the verb آورید, which is the polite/plural imperative (or classical-form imperative) of آوردن 'to bring'. Word order (object — locative — verb) is normal in poetic/older Persian for emphasis: the object and location are fronted and the command is given with آورید.
segments.s176-g186: کمان و کمند و کلاه آورید is a continuation of the imperative, listing the specific gifts. The conjunction و simply links the items in a coordinated list (bow, lasso/snare, helmet/hat). Again آورید is the same plural/polite imperative commanding that these be brought; the verb appears after the list (verb-final), typical in Persian poetry and prose.
segments.s176-g187: بدو گفت گیو‌ای سگ بی‌خرد uses the older/transposed order 'بدو گفت' = 'to him he said' (بدو = 'to him/unto him', placed before the verb), followed by the speaker's name with a vocative particle گیو‌ای (Giv + ای), then the insult سگ بی‌خرد ('foolish dog' — سَگ used figuratively). گفت here is the narrative past 'he said'; the short noun phrase 'سگ بی‌خرد' is direct address.
segments.s176-g188: توگفتی که این آب مردم خورد is an embedded quotation introduced without punctuation: توگفتی ('you said') + که ('that') introduces the content clause. این آب ('this water') is the subject, مردم is a plural noun 'people' modifying the role of consumers, and خورد is the past form of خوردن used here in the sense 'is/was consumed' (i.e., 'for people to drink'). The clause reads 'you said that this water is consumed by/for the people' (i.e., fit for people to drink).
segments.s176-g189: چنین مایه ور پرهنر شهریار is a nominal phrase describing the king: چنین ('such/so') modifies the compound descriptors مایه‌ور پرهنر شهریار. مایه‌ور is a compound meaning 'rich/wealthy' (مایه = resource/means + -ور as a formative), پرهنر = 'full of skill'/'highly skilled', and شهریار = 'king/lord'. The adjectives precede the head noun, as is typical.
segments.s176-g190: همی از تو کشتی کند خواستار is a verbal clause with an archaic iterative/progressive marker همی (meaning 'continually' or 'will') followed by the preposition از ('from'), the agent/possessor تو ('you'), the object کشتی ('ship'), and the verb phrase کند خواستار. کند is the classical 3rd-person singular present/potential of کردن used as the light verb, and خواستار here (literally 'desirous') functions as the complement meaning 'to request/demand' — together 'will demand (a ship) from you'.
segments.s176-g191: ندادی کنون هدیه تو مباد is a compact clause with the negative past verb ندادی ('you did not give') followed by the temporal adverb کنون ('now') and the object هدیه تو ('your gift'). The final مباد is an optative/negative wish meaning 'may it not be' — classical Persian places this wish after the clause to curse or hope that the stated situation does not obtain.
segments.s176-g192: بود روز کاین روزت آید به یاد is a predictive/explanatory clause: بود here is the copula 'there is/there will be' governing روز ('a day'), followed by a relative clause کاین روزت آید به یاد ('that this day will come to your remembrance'). آید is the present/future third-person singular of آمدن 'to come' and به یاد ('to mind/in memory') is the prepositional phrase indicating that the day will come to be remembered by you. Overall: 'there will be a day when this day comes to your memory.'
segments.s176-g25: سپهبد is the title 'the spahbed' (a military commander) and functions as the subject of چو گفت; چو + past verb (چو گفت) is a temporal clause meaning 'when he spoke'. سپهرم is a proper name in the subject position of the main clause and شنید is a simple past verb 'heard' (third person). The clause order is subordinate temporal clause (چو گفت) followed by the main clause (سپهرم شنید).
segments.s176-g26: سپاهی here denotes a group/host ('an army' or 'troops') and ز پیش is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from ahead / from the front'. اندر آمد پدید is a verbal phrase: اندر آمد 'came in/forward' and پدید (postpositive) emphasizes 'appeared' or 'came into view'. Together they mean 'an army appeared from ahead.'
segments.s176-g27: سپهدار is the title 'commander' and is followed by پیران (here a name/title or epithet) identifying him; به پیش اندرون is a locative phrase 'into the fore / into the presence' with به + پیش (toward / before) and اندرون 'inside/inner place'. The whole segment means 'the commander Piran went forward into the inner presence/forecourt.'
segments.s176-g28: سرو روی is a compound epithet 'cypress-faced' (a poetic way to describe an upright/tall or beautiful face), و links the next noun یالش 'his mane' (hair). همه 'all' quantifies both items, and پر ز خون is a fixed phrase 'full of blood' (پر = 'full (of)'). Word order places the quantified nouns before the adjective phrase 'پر ز خون.'
segments.s176-g29: گمان برد is an idiomatic past-tense phrase meaning 'he supposed / he guessed' (گمان + past verb برد). کاو here is the fused archaic relative/pronoun 'که او' = 'that he' introducing the subordinate clause; گیو is the proper name 'Giv' and رایافتست is an archaic past form meaning 'found/obtained' (third person). The whole segment reads 'he supposed that Giv had found (it).'
segments.s176-g30: به پیروزی 'toward victory' is a prepositional phrase headed by به + noun, از پیش is an adverbial 'from the front / forward / in advance', and بشتافتست is an archaic past-tense verb 'hastened' (he rushed). Together they mean 'he rushed forward toward victory.'
segments.s176-g31: چو نزدیکتر شد forms a temporal subordinate clause 'when he became/grew nearer' (چو = 'when', نزدیکتر شد = 'became nearer'). نگه کرد شاه is inverted verb-before-subject order typical in poetry: نگه کرد 'made a look / looked' and شاه is the subject 'the king' — 'the king looked.'
segments.s176-g32: چنان خسته بد is an adjectival phrase 'so / such (چنان) badly exhausted (خسته بد)' where بد functions adverbially 'badly' or 'severely'. پهلوان سپاه is a noun phrase 'the champion of the army' (pahlavān + genitive noun). Combined: 'the army's champion was so badly exhausted.'
segments.s176-g49: همانا is an emphatic particle ('truly/indeed') introducing a statement. که here is the subordinating conjunction 'that', beginning the clause. باران (noun) + نبارد (negative present/present-subjunctive of باریدن) together mean 'rain does not fall'; ز is the poetic form of از ('from'), and میغ is the agent/source 'blade' — the prepositional phrase ز میغ ('from the blade') follows the verb in this poetic order.
segments.s176-g50: فزون functions adverbially here ('more/indeed'), and زانک is the causal conjunction 'for/since'. بارید is the simple past 'rained/fell' (here used metaphorically of a sword). بر introduces the target 'on/upon', سر + ش is 'his head' (ش is the clitic possessive), and تیغ is the noun 'sword' — the phrase بر سرش تیغ follows the verb, a poetic inversion of a more conversational تیغ بر سرش بارید.
segments.s176-g51: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. اندر گلستان is a locative phrase 'in the rose-garden'. به زین بر بخفت is a somewhat inverted, poetic expression: به + زین ('to/onto the saddle') with the adverbial particle بر and the past verb بخفت ('he slept' — archaic past), yielding 'he slept on (his) saddle.' The verb comes after its locative phrase here for poetic effect.
segments.s176-g52: تو گفتی is an address 'you said' (گفتی is second-person past). که introduces reported content ('that'). گشتست is a compound/archaic perfect-like form ('has become' / 'it has turned out'), with با کوه جفت as its complement: با 'with' + کوه 'mountain' + جفت 'pair/match' — i.e. 'became paired/matched with the mountain.' Word order places the verb before its complement in this poetic line.
segments.s176-g53: سرانجام is an adverb 'finally/at last'. برگشت here functions as the past verb 'returned' (3rd person). یکسر qualifies سپاه as 'the whole/entire army'. The verb precedes the noun phrase in this poetic ordering but should be read as 'the entire army returned.'
segments.s176-g54: بجز من is the prepositional phrase 'except me'. نشد is used impersonally/past ('did not happen' / 'there was not'), introducing the negative existential idea. پیش او is a locative phrase 'before/in the presence of him', and کینه‌خواه is a compound noun 'one who desires grudge/vengeance' (کینه 'hatred/grudge' + خواه 'desirer') — together meaning 'there was no one before him desiring vengeance, except me.'
segments.s176-g55: گریزان is a present participle/adjective 'fleeing' or 'on the run'. ز من is the poetic preposition 'from me'. تاب‌داده is a past-participle-type modifier ('having given a twist/looped' or 'looped/formed') qualifying کمند, so تاب‌داده کمند means 'a looped / well-formed lasso/snare' — a compound descriptive phrase placed before the noun it modifies.
segments.s176-g56: بیفگند is the past verb 'he cast/threw' (from افکندن) applied to the snare. و is the coordinating 'and', آمد is the past 'came'. میانم literally 'into my midst' is میان + possessive -م, here meaning 'into my midst/among me.' به بند is a prepositional phrase 'into/under fetters' (بند 'fetter/bond'), indicating that as a result he ended up bound. The sequence describes a fast-action chain: he threw the snare and consequently came into bondage among/near the speaker.
segments.s176-g65: مرا داد زین‌گونه سوگند سخت is literal word order ‘gave (داد) an oath (سوگند) to me (مرا) such/like this (زین‌گونه) severe (سخت)’. In Classical Persian دادن + سوگند = “to swear (to someone)”; مَرا (م + را) is the 1st‑person object ‘to me’. Adjectives follow the noun (سوگند سخت).
segments.s176-g66: بخوردم چو دیدم که برگشت بخت is parsed as two clauses: بخوردم (1st‑person past) ‘I was consumed/overwhelmed’ and the temporal clause چو دیدم که برگشت بخت ‘when I saw that fortune/lot had turned’. چو introduces the time clause; دیدم is past ‘I saw’; که introduces the content 'that' clause; برگشت بخت is a nominal phrase ‘the turning/reversal of fortune’.
segments.s176-g67: که کس را نگویی که بگشای دست is a complex subordinate clause introduced by که. کس را نگویی literally 'you would not / is not told to anyone' — نگویی here functions as a negative/optative verb form (classical flavor) meaning 'one would not say/urge'. The second که introduces the infinitival/requestive clause 'that (one) open (بگشای) the hand (دست)'; بگشای دست is an idiom meaning 'to put forth help/extend a hand'.
segments.s176-g68: چنین رو دمان تا بجای نشست: چنین 'thus/so' modifies the following phrase; رو دمان is a compact, poetic NP (رو = appearance/face or turn; دمان = دامان, 'skirt/hem') describing posture or clothing folded about him. تا + verb (نشست) = 'until he sat (in place)'; بجای نشست = 'sat into his place/seat'. The clause as a whole describes an action lasting until he was seated.
segments.s176-g69: ندانم چه رازست نزد سپهر is a straightforward clause: ندانم = 'I do not know' (ندهان = negated first‑person of دانستن), چه = 'what', رازست = 'is a secret' (راز + است fused), and نزد سپهر = 'with/in the presence of the heavens' (نزد + سپهر). The verbless/nominal copula construction (رازست) is typical in poetry.
segments.s176-g70: بخواهد بریدن ز ما پاک مهر: بخواهد is 3rd‑person present/future 'it wishes/will', followed by the infinitive بریدن 'to cut/sever' — Persian often uses بخواهد + infinitive to express desire or intention. ز ما = 'from us', پاک مهر = 'pure love/affection' (پاک modifying مهر). The whole means the heavens (implied by preceding clause) may wish to sever our pure affection.
segments.s176-g71: چو بشنید گفتارش افراسیاب: چو 'when', بشنید past 'he heard', گفتارش 'his speech/utterance' (گفتار + ـش), and the name افراسیاب appears after the clause as subject/topic — word order is flexible in poetry, but functionally this reads 'When Afrasiab heard his words'.
segments.s176-g72: بدیده ز خشم اندرآورد آب is idiomatic: بدیده (here part of the phrase 'به دیده') indicates 'in the eyes', ز خشم 'from/with anger', and اندرآورد آب = 'brought forth water' (i.e. tears welled up). اندرآورد is past tense 'caused to bring in/bring forth'; آب (water) here figuratively = tears. The clause describes anger filling his eyes and producing tears.
segments.s176-g57: پراگنده شد is a past-result predicate: پراگنده (an adjective 'scattered') precedes the auxiliary/past verb شد ('became'/'was made'), so the clause means 'became scattered'. دانش و هوش من is a possessive compound noun phrase ('my knowledge and my intellect') with من as the possessive pronoun clitic; the verb is applied to that whole phrase. The inverted order (adjective + شد + subject) is a poetic word order.
segments.s176-g58: به خاک اندر آمد is a verbal phrase meaning 'came down/to the dust' where به + خاک gives the goal/ground ('to the dust') and اندر آمد ('came in/arrived') is an archaic/poetic periphrasis for 'fell' or 'came down'. سر و دوش من is a coordinated possessive noun ('my head and shoulder(s)') following the verb; Persian often allows the verb before the subject in poetry, so the subject (سر و دوش من) comes after آمد.
segments.s176-g59: از اسپ اندر آمد is a short clause meaning 'he came down from the steed' (dismounted), where از marks source ('from') and اندر آمد is the verbal 'came/arrived' in poetic diction. دو دستم ببست has an implied 3rd-person subject ('he') omitted—دو modifies the possessed noun دستم ('my two hands') and ببست is the past tense 'bound/tied' (3rd person). The two clauses are concatenated without an explicit conjunction, a common narrative compression in poetry.
segments.s176-g60: برافگند is the past tense 'threw/cast' (here transitive, object omitted but understood from context: 'threw [me]'). بر زین is a locative phrase 'upon/onto the saddle' (بر = on/upon, زین = saddle). و خود بر نشست combines و (and) with خود (emphatic 'himself') and نشست ('sat down'); here برنشست means 'sat upon it'—the clausal sequence describes successive actions (threw onto the saddle, and he himself sat on it).
segments.s176-g61: زمانی here functions as an adverbial meaning 'for a while' or 'at one time'. سر وپایم is a coordinated possessive noun ('my head and my feet' i.e. my whole body) and اندر کمند uses اندر (in) + کمند (lasso/snare) to indicate being trapped. There is no explicit copula 'was'—the state is expressed existentially by placing the noun phrase with the locative.
segments.s176-g62: به دیگر زمان is a temporal phrase 'at another time' (به + دیگر + زمان). زیر سوگند و بند uses زیر ('under', meaning 'subject to' or 'under the power of') with سوگند ('oath') and بند ('bond, fetter') to indicate being bound both by oath and by physical bonds; again the copula is omitted and the state is implied.
segments.s176-g63: به introduces an oath-formula here and takes the sense 'by' (instrumental/oblative 'I swear by ...'). جان و سر شاه و خورشید و ماه is a paratactic list of sacred/royal referents ('the life and head of the king and the sun and the moon')—جان and سر form a pair of emphatic body-related oaths, and the sequence names the objects sworn by. Word order is listing/accumulative rather than syntactically headed.
segments.s176-g64: به again marks the oath ('by'). دادار هرمزد is an appositive/title + name ('the Creator, Ahura Mazda'), and تخت و کلاه lists regalia ('throne and hat/crown'); کلاه is literally 'hat' but here functions metonymically as 'crown'. The whole segment completes the series of sacred/royal items invoked in the oath.
segments.s176-g201: چنین (an adverb) appears first to mark the manner 'thus/so'; داد is the past tense verb 'gave' (he gave); پاسخ is the direct object 'answer/reply' placed after the verb — a common poetic word order. که‌ای شهریار is the opening of quoted speech with که + ای (vocative) addressing the king: 'O king.'
segments.s176-g202: پدر باژبان بود is a simple past clause: 'my father was a bugler' (بود is the past copula). The conjunction و links the next nominal phrase 'من باژدار' where the copula is omitted (typical in Persian), so it reads 'and I (am/was) a bugler' by ellipsis of the verb.
segments.s176-g203: ندیدم is the past negative 'I did not see'; نه هرگز شنیدم is a coordinated negative expression 'nor ever did I hear' (هرگز = 'ever/never', شنیدم = 'I heard'), and چنین ('such') refers forward to the action being described. The two negatives form a rhetorical negation: 'neither saw nor ever heard of such.'
segments.s176-g204: که introduces a relative clause 'that/who'; کردی here is a past tense verb 'did' (used relative/indefinite in older/poetic usage); کسی is the indefinite 'someone/anyone'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from', linking آب جیحون ('the water of Jeyhun') to زمین ('the land/earth') — i.e. 'from the waters of Jeyhun to the land.'
segments.s176-g205: بهاران و این آب lists two items ('the springs/this spring and this water') linked by و. با موج تیز is an instrumental/prepositional phrase 'with sharp/swift waves' modifying آب; adjective تیز follows موج as usual in poetry.
segments.s176-g206: چو functions temporally 'when'; اندر شوی is an archaic/poetic phrasing where اندر = 'into/within' and شوی is 2nd-person singular present/subjunctive 'you enter' (used generically here: 'when one enters it'). نیست introduces the existential negation 'there is not'; راه گریز is the noun phrase 'a way of escape' — overall: 'when one enters, there is no way of escape.'
segments.s176-g207: چنان (thus/so) modifies the verb برگذشتند, which is the past tense 3rd-person plural 'they passed by.' هر سه سوار is a classical way to say 'all three horsemen' (هر + numeral can indicate each/all in older usage), with the noun سوار 'rider/horseman' following.
segments.s176-g208: تو گفتی is the past of گفتن 'you said' (introducing reported speech); هوا داشت is an idiom 'to have a disposition/desire' (here 'they had a mind/wish'), with داشت as the past auxiliary/verb 'had.' شان is the 3rd-person plural possessive 'their' referring to the horsemen; برکنار ('aside/apart') is a predicate complement: 'they had a mind to stand apart.' Note the inversion and elliptical style typical of epic diction.
segments.s176-g209: "ازان پس" is an adverbial phrase meaning 'after that/thereupon' placed before the verb; "بفرمود" is the courtly past-tense verb 'he said/commanded' (honorific) and takes the quoted clause introduced by "که"; the name "افراسیاب" follows as the agent (subject) in an inverted, formal word order.
segments.s176-g210: Here "که" introduces reported speech/command; "بشتاب" is an imperative 'hurry!' and is joined by the coordinating "و" to a second command. "کشتی" (ship) appears before the verbal infinitival/causative "برافگن" ('set afloat/throw into'), so the object precedes the verb; "به آب" is a prepositional phrase 'into the water' indicating direction.
segments.s176-g211: "بدو" is a prepositional/clitic form meaning 'to him' ('said to him'); "گفت" is the speech verb 'said' (here used with inverted word order, which is common), with the speaker's name "هومان" following; "که‌ای شهریار" functions as the beginning of the quoted address—an archaic vocative 'O king' introduced by the particle "که" + the interjectional "ای/ـای".
segments.s176-g212: "براندیش" is an imperative meaning 'consider/reconsider' or 'reflect'; it is followed by coordinating "و" and then the negative imperative construction "آتش مکن" ('do not make/set fire'); the locative phrase "در کنار" ('at/on the bank/edge') specifies where the action must not be done.
segments.s176-g213: Standard subject–prepositional phrase–predicate order: "تو" (you) + comitative "با این سواران" (with these riders) + directional "به ایران" (to Iran) and the verb "شوی" (2nd-person singular of شدن). Here "شوی" functions as a future/resultive 'you will be/go' (classical Persian uses this present-subjunctive form to indicate future/hypothetical result).
segments.s176-g214: "همی" is an emphatic/adverbial particle marking immediacy or emphasis; the idiom "در دم" means 'in a moment/at once'; "گاوشیدان" is a nominal (cowherd/dairyman) used here as a degrading future state, and again "شوی" states the result 'you will become.' The whole clause predicts rapid humiliation: 'you will soon become a cowherd.'
segments.s176-g215: The particle "چو" means 'as/like' and introduces a series of comparisons; here it precedes the proper names "گودرز" and (again with "چون") "رستم" followed by the epithet/place-name "پیلتن" (Rostam 'of Piltan'). The structure is parallel listing: 'as Goudarz and as Rostam of Piltan.'
segments.s176-g216: Continuation of the same parallelism: "چو طوس" ('as Tous'—here "طوس" is the hero Tous, not the city) and "چو گرگین" ('as Gorgin'), followed by the demonstrative "آن" + "انجمن" ('that assembly/group'). The final phrase points to the gathered company mentioned earlier; syntax is a coordinated list of similes or examples.
segments.s176-g169: بدان here is a prepositional phrase meaning 'to that' (ب = to/into + دان as a demonstrative/old formation), followed by نیستان 'reed‑bed' as the place. در نیایش گرفت is a compound where در + noun (نیایش) with گرفت (past of گرفتن) means 'took to prayer' or 'began praying' — گرفت functions as a light verb meaning 'took up / began'. Word order is locative/prepositional phrase then action.
segments.s176-g170: جهان‌آفرین is a compound noun 'world‑creator / Creator' (object of praise). را is the definite object marker marking ستایش as a direct object. ستایش گرفت repeats the light‑verb pattern (noun + گرفتن) meaning 'took up praise' or 'began to praise'; گرفت again is the past tense 'took/started'.
segments.s176-g171: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. از رود is a motion phrase 'from / across the river' and کردند (past, 3rd pl.) + گذر (noun 'passage / crossing') forms 'they made the crossing'; هر سه ('all three') identifies the participants. The construction is literal 'when from the river they made, the three, a passage' — typical SOV/PV flexibility in classical Persian.
segments.s176-g172: نگهبان کشتی is a genitive phrase 'the guard (keeper) of the ship' (possessor + possessed). شد is the past of شدن 'became', and آسیمه سر is a compound adjective meaning 'distraught / frantic' (آسیمه + سر functioning together as a predicate). Word order: subject phrase → copula → predicative adjective.
segments.s176-g173: به یاران is an indirect object/dative 'to the companions'. چنین گفت is 'said thus' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = past of گفتن 'said'); کاینت شگفت is an exclamatory clause: کاینت (کاین + ت) 'this/that which / how this' and شگفت 'marvelous, astonishing' — together functioning as an interjection 'How wondrous this is!'.
segments.s176-g174: کزین = 'from this' (کز + این), برتر 'higher / superior' modifies اندیشه 'thought'. نتوان is the negative potential 'cannot' (present/past-modal form used impersonally), and گرفت here is the light/passive sense 'be taken / be conceived'. The clause means 'from this no higher thought can be taken' — i.e., 'nothing superior can be conceived from this.'
segments.s176-g175: This is a triadic list of places/streams: بهاران (Bahārān: either 'the springs' or a place/seasonal name), و جیحون (the river Jeyhūn/Amu Darya), و آب روان 'and flowing water' (آب + روان, here روان = 'flowing'). The coordinating و simply links the items; order is typical listing.
segments.s176-g176: سه (three) quantifies the following nouns: جوشنور is a poetic/compound noun (here 'radiant surge / burst of light'), و اسپ 'and steeds/horses' adds to the list, و برگستوان is a final coordinated item; برگستوان in form suggests 'return' or a technical/onomastic term. The phrase is a catalogue of three types of dazzling/military/ceremonial things: 'three jošnūrs, horses, and bargastavān.'
segments.s176-g9: A compact noun phrase followed by the narrative past verb. همه (modifier) precedes مرز لشکر (a genitive/compound meaning 'the front/ranks of the army'), پراگنده is a postpositive adjective meaning 'scattered', and دید is the past tense verb 'saw' that closes the clause. The object ('them' = the ranks/men) is not repeated; the participial adjective describes their state which the subject perceived.
segments.s176-g10: This clause begins with the locative phrase به هر جای ('to/at every place'), then the prepositional phrase بر مردم ('upon the people'), and ends with the past-periphrastic sight construction افگنده دید. افگنده is a past participle (from افکندن 'to cast/throw') and together with دید indicates 'he saw (them) cast/thrown' — i.e. he perceived that they had been cast upon the people.
segments.s176-g11: بپرسید is the narrative past 'they asked'. کاین is a contracted sequence (که این) introducing the embedded question/relative clause 'this champion', and با سپاه ('with his host') is a prepositional phrase modifying پهلوان. Word order is verb (past) + clause; the clause identifies the person being asked about.
segments.s176-g12: This is the remainder of the embedded question. کی is the interrogative 'who' here, آمد is the simple past 'came', ز is the poetic preposition 'from', ایران is the place 'Iran', and بدین رزمگاه is the contracted locative 'to this battlefield' (ب + دین = بدین). The clause asks the identity of the one who came from Iran to this battlefield.
segments.s176-g13: A compact noun phrase: نبرد ('battle') followed by آگهی کس ('news/knowledge of anyone') and the source phrase ز جنگ‌آوران ('from among the warriors'). The sequence functions to say that 'no one among the fighters had knowledge of the battle' — Persian poetry often omits an explicit negative particle if implied by surrounding sense; syntactically this is a noun cluster indicating the lack/absence of news among the warriors.
segments.s176-g14: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'that/which', بگذشت is the past intransitive 'passed by/went past', زین سان is the idiomatic adverbial 'in this way/so', and سپاهی گران is a noun phrase 'a heavy/weighty host' (سپاهی = host/army, گران = heavy/formidable). The clause explains what news was lacking: that such a great host had passed by.
segments.s176-g15: که again introduces the clause; برد here functions as 'carried/took' in the narrative sense (the exact lexical entry provided does not list this sense, so it is treated as a contextually determined verb), and آگهی is 'news/information'. نزد means 'to/in the presence of', آن is the demonstrative 'that', and دیوزاد is 'demon‑born/fiend'. Together: 'which carried the news to that demon‑born.'
segments.s176-g16: This clause is a wish or jussive negation ended by مباد ('may it not be'). که introduces the content, کس را is 'to/for no one' (کس = anyone/someone, را is the object marker), دل و مغز پیران is 'the heart and brain of the elders', and مباد negates as a formulaic 'may it not be' — literally 'may there not be to anyone the heart and brain of the elders', i.e. a wish that none possess/retain those (contextually a curse/prayer).
segments.s176-g129: کنون is an adverb meaning 'now' and opens the clause. آب ما را is noun + possessor + object marker (آب + ما + را) = 'our water' with را marking it as definite/direct object or topic; و links the next item. کشتی ترا is 'your ship' where ت is the enclitic 2nd-person possessive attached to کشتی and را (written after) marks it as definite/object. The sequence lists two items (our water and your ship) as the things being spoken about.
segments.s176-g130: بدین گونه is a fixed phrase 'به + این گونه' meaning 'in this manner / thus' (here written as ب + دین + گونه), where ب (به) is the prepositional prefix and دین represents این in this orthography. شاهی درشتی‌ت را is a nominal phrase: شاهی ('kingship, kingliness') modifying درشتی ('rudeness, roughness'), with the enclitic ت (your) attaching to درشتی and را marking it as definite/object. There is no overt finite verb in this segment; the clause functions as an exclamatory nominal comment: 'with such kingliness, your rudeness.'
segments.s176-g131: بدو گفت گیو is inverted word order common in epic Persian: بدو ('to him') fronted, گفت (said) is the reporting verb, and گیو is the speaker (Giv) — 'Giv said to him.' ار is the conditional particle 'if'; تو کیخسروی addresses the addressee 'you, Keykhosrow' (کیخسروی is the name/title used vocatively here).
segments.s176-g132: نبینی is the negative present/subjunctive form used in a conditional introduced by ار: 'if you do not see / if you should not see.' ازین is 'از + این' = 'from this' (written as از + ین here), so ازین آب = 'from this water.' جز نیکوی = 'except (that which is) good' — the two words set up the idea 'see nothing in this water except goodness.'
segments.s176-g133: فریدون که بگذاشت is a subordinate clause: فریدون (Fereydun) + که (introducing the clause 'when/that') + بگذاشت (past 'he placed/put/appointed'). اروند رود is a compound name 'Arvand River' (river name + noun 'رود'), the patient/thing Fereydun acted on. Together: 'When Fereydun placed/appointed the Arvand River...'.
segments.s176-g134: فرستاد is the past tense 'he sent.' تخت مهی را is the definite object 'the moon-like throne' (تخت + مهی as a descriptive compound, with را marking it). درود is the thing sent: 'a greeting' or 'salutation.' Word order is verb + object (with را) + sent-item: 'he sent a greeting to the moon-like throne.'
segments.s176-g135: جهانی شد is 'became (a) world' or 'a world came to be;' او را uses را after the pronoun to mark 'for him' (benefactive/experiential). سراسر رهی literally 'an entire/path throughout' — i.e. 'the whole world became a path for him' (سراسر modifies the extent, رهی = 'a way/road'). The construction expresses that the whole world opened up as a path before him.
segments.s176-g136: که introduces a relative clause modifying 'رهی': 'which/that was with light and with nobility.' با روشنی بود = 'was with brightness/light'; و با فرهی = 'and with nobility/grandeur' (فرهی is an adjective/noun meaning cultured, noble). بود is the past copula 'was'; repeated با emphasizes accompanying qualities.
segments.s176-g89: 'که' begins a subordinate clause here (equivalent to 'when/that'); 'چون' functions as a temporal conjunction ('when' / 'as') introducing the time clause. 'گیو و خسرو' is the coordinated subject (name + and + name) and 'ز جیحون گذشت' places the prepositional phrase 'from/across the Jeyhun' before the verb; 'گذشت' is a past simple third-person singular (classical word order: object/PP before verb).
segments.s176-g90: The phrase 'غم و رنج ما' is a possessive noun phrase ('our sorrow and suffering'); 'باد' is the optative/volitive particle meaning 'may' (used to express a wish or curse), followed by 'گردد' (classical present/subjunctive 'become' / 'come to be'), and 'بدشت' is a locative complement ('into/on the plain'). Word order places the wish-particle + verb after the subject phrase, producing 'may our grief become (on) the plain.'
segments.s176-g91: 'نشان آمد' is a simple predicate ('a sign came' / 'a sign appeared'); 'از گفته راستان' is a genitive/ablative phrase 'from the words/statements of the righteous' with 'از' introducing source. Note the classical noun-before-possessor ordering: 'گفته راستان' = 'the words of the righteous.'
segments.s176-g92: 'که' introduces the reported content ('that/which'); 'دانا' is here used as a noun ('the wise one' / sage) and 'بگفت' is the classical past-tense form with the historical prefix 'ب-' ('he said' / 'had said'). 'از گه باستان' is a temporal phrase ('from/ since the olden time(s)') where 'گه' = 'time/occasion' and 'باستان' = 'ancient.'
segments.s176-g93: 'که' again introduces a content clause ('that'); 'از تخمه تور' = 'from the stock/offspring of Tur' where 'تخمه' means 'offspring' and 'تور' is the proper name Tūr. 'وز کیقباد' in the manuscript strings together elements meaning 'and/of Kay Qobad' — the particle 'وز' here functions to link to 'کیقباد' but its listed lexical senses do not match this conjunctive use (see senses mapping).
segments.s176-g94: 'یکی شاه خیزد' = 'one king will spring up' with 'یکی' (one/a certain) modifying 'شاه' and 'خیزد' the verb 'arise/spring up' in third-person (classical present/future sense). 'ز هر دو نژاد' is a prepositional origin phrase ('from each/both of the two lineages'), where 'هر دو' idiomatically means 'both' and 'نژاد' = 'lineage.'
segments.s176-g95: 'که' introduces the clause again; 'توران زمین را' uses 'را' as the definite object marker attached to 'زمین' ('the land of Turan' as definite object), and 'کند' is the present/simple classical third-person verb 'makes/does.' 'خارستان' is the result-noun ('a thorny thicket' or figuratively 'wasteland') and functions as object/predicative complement: 'makes the land into a wasteland.'
segments.s176-g96: 'نماند' is a non‑past/gnomic negative form meaning 'will not remain/there will be none left' (third-person); 'برین بوم' = 'on this soil' where 'بر' = 'on/upon' + 'ین' (orthographic variant of 'این' = 'this'), 'بوم' = 'homeland/soil.' The coordinated phrase 'و بر شارستان' adds 'and upon the towns/cities' ('شارستان' = towns). Together: 'nothing will remain on this soil or in its cities.'
segments.s176-g97: رسیدند is a past-tense, 3rd-person plural verb 'they arrived/reached' placed at the start (typical of epic Persian). پس is an adverb 'then' following the verb. The two proper names گیو و خسرو are the persons who arrived. بر آب is a prepositional phrase 'onto/at the water' (بر + noun), giving the location reached.
segments.s176-g98: همی is an emphatic/progressive particle (classical usage) giving a continuous or emphatic nuance; بودشان is the past copula with the 3rd-person plural pronominal suffix -شان 'they were/had'. بر + infinitive (گذشتن) marks the object/purpose 'to cross', and شتاب is a noun 'haste' that functions as the complement: literally 'they had haste to cross.' Word order places the copula before the verbal noun phrase.
segments.s176-g99: گرفتند is a past, 3rd-person plural verb 'they seized/took'; پیگار here is a substantive meaning 'battle/engagement' used as the object ('they took up battle') so the phrase means 'they engaged/fought' or 'they seized (the matter of) battle' with the following با + agent. با باژخواه marks the adversary 'with the toll-seeker.'
segments.s176-g100: که introduces the content of their question or speech ('that/which'); کشتی is the noun 'ship'; کدامست is an interrogative copula 'which is it?' where کدام modifies کشتی and ست is the old copula 'is' placed after the question word. بر باژگاه (بر + باژگاه) indicates the location 'at/on the toll-house.' The whole clause thus reports their asking which ship was at the toll-house.
segments.s176-g101: نوندی is used as a proper name (here the ship's name) or a rare term called out at the start; کجا is an exclamatory/interrogative particle here ('how/what'), fronted for emphasis. بادبانش is 'its sail' (بادبان + possessive suffix -ش) and نکوست is the predicative adjective 'is good/beautiful' (نکو + ست 'is'), so the clause praises the sail: 'what a fine sail it has.'
segments.s176-g102: به خوبی is an adverbial phrase 'in goodness/very well'; سزاوار is an adjective/participle 'befitting/appropriate for' and is followed by the beneficiary کیخسرو (Kay Khosrow). اوست is the copula 'it is' with the resumptive pronoun او 'it/he', so the clause states that the ship's fine quality is fitting for Kay Khosrow.
segments.s176-g103: چنین 'thus/so' modifies گفت (here the verb 'said'); با گیو marks the addressee 'to Giv' (با in older usage often signals the person concerned), پس is temporal 'then', and باج‌خواه is the speaker/topic 'the toll-seeker' (placed after the verb as common in classical word order). The phrase reports that the toll-seeker then said these words to Giv.
segments.s176-g104: که introduces the reported content 'that'; آب روان را is 'the running water' with را marking the definite/affected object/topic. The paired چه ... چه ... is a correlative meaning 'whether ... or ...' (here 'whether servant or king'), so چه چاکر چه شاه means 'whether [he is] a retainer/servant or a king.' The clause sets up the contrast about the person at the river.
segments.s176-g177: بدین ژرف دریا چنین بگذرد is a poetic word order: بدین (به + این = 'by/with this, thus') + ژرف دریا ('the deep sea') forms a circumstantial phrase 'through/by this deep sea', followed by چنین بگذرد ('thus he passes'). بگذرد is a 3rd singular present/past‑narrative form (classical narrative/optative flavor) of گذراندن/گذاشتن meaning 'passes / goes through'.
segments.s176-g178: خردمندش از مردمان نشمرد: خردمند (the wise man) plus the enclitic ش (here referring to 'him'/'his' in classical usage) is the noun phrase; از مردمان ('from/out of the people') is a partitive/contrastive prepositional phrase, and نشمرد is the negative past/preterite of شمردن 'to count/ reckon' meaning 'did not count/ reckon (him)'.
segments.s176-g179: پشیمان شد is a two‑word predicate: پشیمان ('regretful') + شد (past of شدن 'became'), so 'he became regretful'. از کار و گفتار خویش is a prepositional phrase 'about/from his deeds and speech'; خویش is formed from خوی + enclitic ش and here functions as the possessive 'his (own)'.
segments.s176-g180: تبه دید is a light verbal construction where تبه ('bad/ruinous') is predicated and دید (past of دیدن) means 'he saw/considered (it)'. ازان ('from that/thereby') gives the reason/source ('because of that act'), and کار بازار خویش = 'his market/business' (کار بازار = 'business/market affairs') with خویش again the possessive.
segments.s176-g181: بیاراست کشتی is a perfective/past verb 'he equipped/trimmed the ship' (بیاراست = بی + آراستن / 'made ready/arrayed'). به چیزی که داشت is a prepositional phrase 'with something that he had' where که داشت is a relative clause 'that he had'. The object کشتی follows the verb in this poetic order.
segments.s176-g182: ز باد هوا ('because of/from the wind and the air/weather') is an ablative/reason phrase (ز = from/because of). بادبان برگذاشت: بادبان 'the sail' is object and برگذاشت is a past verb meaning 'put aside / left off / took down' (برگذاشت used here as he abandoned or removed the sail because of the wind).
segments.s176-g183: به پوزش برفت is 'he went to (ask) pardon' where به + پوزش indicates the purpose or motion 'to ask pardon' and برفت is an archaic/poetic past form of رفتن meaning 'he went' (here with the prefix بر-). از پس شهریار means 'after/behind the king' (از پس = following/after).
segments.s176-g184: چو آمد is a temporal clause 'when he came' (چو = چو/چنانچه = when). به نزدیکی رودبار = 'to/into the vicinity/nearby of Rudbar' (نزديکی = nearness, رودبار = a river‑valley place‑name or 'riverbank').
segments.s176-g137: چه اندیشی is an interrogative phrase (literally "what do you think"); اندیشی is 2nd‑person singular present (you think). ار is the conditional conjunction "if/supposing" that introduces the condition. شاه ایران توی places the noun phrase "king of Iran" before the postposition توی, which here means "among/in the company of." The word order (noun + توی) is typical of older/poetic Persian.
segments.s176-g138: This is a coordinated noun phrase where توی ('among/in') applies to both coordinated nouns. سرنامداران (a formal/poetic plural) means "the foremost/leading men," و is the coordinating 'and', and شیران is a poetic plural (literally 'lions') used metaphorically for "brave men/warriors." The trailing توی repeats the locative/associative marker.
segments.s176-g139: به + (بد آب را) is a prepositional phrase 'to/for that bad water' with را marking آب as definite/ topical. کی بود is an interrogative past copula 'when/ever was there', and راه is the noun 'road/path' that forms the predicate; بر + تو functions here as 'for you'/'toward you' (بر تو راه = 'a way for you'). The overall sequence is a rhetorical question in past tense.
segments.s176-g140: که introduces a relative clause 'which/that'. با governs a list of qualities: فر (royal glory), و linking, برزی (an archaic poetic term here paired with فر), and زیبای گاه (literally 'the beauty of the time/occasion'). The phrase describes the thing referred to by the antecedent (the water or the road) in poetic diction.
segments.s176-g141: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. من شوم uses شوم (1st‑person subjunctive/potential) meaning 'if I become' or 'should I become'. غرقه‌گر is an agentive noun 'drowner' and مادرت is 'your mother' with the 2nd‑person enclitic ت attached to مادر to indicate possession. The whole phrase expresses a hypothetical condition ('if I become the one who drowns your mother').
segments.s176-g142: گزندی (an indefinite noun 'harm') is the subject/topic of an impersonal negative statement introduced by نباید ('there should not / must not'). The following که introduces the subordinate clause whose verb is گیرد (present/subjunctive 'seize/take'), and سرت is 'your head' (سر + enclitic ت). The clause means 'there should be no harm that seizes your head.'
segments.s176-g143: ز مادر تو is a source phrase 'from your mother' (ز = 'from' in poetic register) where تو is the addressed person. بودی is the past copula 'you were', and مراد جهان is the predicate 'the desire/wish of the world' (مراد = object of longing, جهان = the world). Word order is origin + subject + copula + predicate, typical in classical Persian.
segments.s176-g144: که introduces an explanatory or result clause. بیکار is an adjective 'idle/unused' modifying تخت ('throne'); بد is an evaluative adjective 'bad/ill' intensifying the state. تخت شاهنشهان ('the throne of the shahanshahs/emperors') is the final noun phrase. Together the clause says the throne of emperors is unoccupied/idle (and thus unfortunate/useless) — a poetic comment on the value or emptiness of power when the ideal (the addressee) is absent.
segments.s176-g121: This chunk is a noun phrase: 'سه دیگر' = 'three others' (numeral + modifier), followed by 'چو' used as 'like/as' and the name-like noun 'شبرنگ بهزاد را' (Shabrang of Behzad) where 'را' marks a definite/affected term. Word order is modifier → comparative → noun phrase; 'را' here functions as the definite/object/topic marker attached to the proper name.
segments.s176-g122: 'که' introduces a relative clause ('which/that'); 'کوتاه دارد' is a classical stative/active construction meaning 'keeps short' or 'holds tight' (دارد = has/keeps). 'به' is the preposition 'to/at', followed by the compound 'تگ باد را' where 'تگ' qualifies and 'باد را' (wind + object marker) forms the object of the prepositional phrase. Overall: 'which he keeps short/held to the wind's (rein/strap)'.
segments.s176-g123: 'چو' here functions as a temporal conjunction 'when'; 'جستی' is an older/polite past-form of جستن ('leapt/sprung') with the subject omitted (classical epic style), 'به' = 'toward/into', and 'خیره زره' = 'dazzling/eye‑striking armor' (خیره modifying زره). The clause describes the action of the fourth man/thing springing at the armor.
segments.s176-g124: 'که' begins a relative/explanatory clause: 'that/which'; 'آن را' = 'it' with the object marker; 'ندانی' is the negative present/subjunctive form of دانستن used here idiomatically as 'you would not / cannot know'; 'گره تا گره' is a repetitive formula meaning 'knot by knot' or 'from knot to knot' (emphasizing tightly interlaced knots). The clause thus stresses that one cannot discern its knots.
segments.s176-g125: 'نگردد' is a negative/habitual form of گردیدن meaning 'will not turn/change' (here passive/impersonal sense), 'چنین' = 'such', 'آهن' = 'iron', and 'از آب‌تر' = 'by/through the (moisture) of water' (آب‌تر here indicating wetness/water). The phrase states that such iron does not change under water.
segments.s176-g126: This is the coordinating negative: 'نه' (not) + 'آتش' (fire). 'برو' here functions as a verbal/adjectival participle 'going/that goes', 'بر' = 'upon/on', 'بود' = past of 'to be' (here 'would be' / 'was'), and 'کارگر' is adjectival 'effective/operative'. Altogether: 'nor would fire that comes upon it be effective.'
segments.s176-g127: A simple negative enumeration: repeated 'نه' ('not/neither') before each weapon noun lists what is excluded — 'نیزه' (spear), 'شمشیر' (sword), 'هندی' qualifies the sword as 'Indian', and 'تیر' (arrow). Word order is the emphatic classical repeated negation.
segments.s176-g128: 'چنین' = 'such/so'; 'باژ' is a poetic noun 'a cry/noise'; 'خواهی' is the 2nd‑person present/future form of خواستن meaning here 'you will/would (attempt to)'; 'ب' + 'دین' together form 'بدین' (= 'in/at this') with 'آب‌گیر' = 'pond/sump' as its complement. The clause asserts that one would (attempt to) raise such a cry at this pool — note: the lemma-sense list gives 'دین' senses that don't match 'this', so that item is irregular here (see senses).
segments.s176-g217: همانا که is an emphatic opening: همانا (verily) + the conjunction/relativizer که introducing the following clause. از گاه سیر آمدی is literal word order 'from the time (گاه) (of) satiety (سیر) you came (آمدی)', where از گاه marks the temporal origin and آمدی is 2nd-person singular past.
segments.s176-g218: The second که repeats the connective introducing the result/clause that follows. ایدر 'here' + به with directional sense 'into/towards' marks motion into the noun چنگال 'claw', شیر is 'lion', and آمدی is again past 2sg; object precedes the verb (poetic word order).
segments.s176-g219: ازین روی is the fixed poetic phrase 'for this reason' (از + این/ین + رو/روی). تا here means 'as far as' (marking extent). چین و ماچین are a paired toponymic/poetic expression meaning distant lands; تراست is a single predicative form 'it is yours' (تَ + راست/تراست) referring to the extent.
segments.s176-g220: A simple nominal list (خور و ماه و کیوان و پروین) serving as the items governed by the single predicate تراست ('are yours'). The conjunction و links the celestial bodies; تراست remains a 3rd-person predicate applying collectively.
segments.s176-g221: تو is the 2nd‑person pronoun; the following nominals توران نگه‌دار و تخت بلند are predicative/titles in apposition ('guardian of Turan' and 'lofty throne'). Persian often omits a copula, so the sequence reads 'you [are] Turan's guardian and [of] the high throne.'
segments.s176-g222: ز ایران uses the poetic preposition ز (= از) 'from' to indicate source; کنون 'now' + نیست 'is not' negates the noun phrase بیم گزند 'fear of harm' — literally 'there is not now a fear of harm from Iran.'
segments.s176-g223: پر از خون دل is an adjectival phrase 'full of the heart's blood' (پر 'full (of)' + از marking the material/content). از رود گشتند باز is a verb phrase: از + رود 'from the river', گشتند is 3rd‑pl past 'they turned/returned', and باز is the adverb 'back/again' — together 'they turned back from the river.'
segments.s176-g224: برآمد is the past/intransitive 'arose' or 'came into being'. برین روزگار دراز is a determiner + noun phrase 'this/that long time/age' (بر + ین used here in older/poetic combination → 'this'), so the clause means 'a long age then arose' or 'thus a long era began.'
segments.s176-g145: This is an older Persian word order where the object 'مرا' (me) appears before the subject and verb: literally 'me also mother for your sake bore'. 'نیز' = 'also' modifies the statement. 'ز بهر' is a poetic phrase meaning 'for the sake of' (ز = because/of; بهر = for). 'زاد' is a past 3rd-person singular verb 'bore/gave birth to'.
segments.s176-g146: 'ازین' is an archaic/poetic form of 'از این' ('of/from this'); here 'ازین کار' = 'concerning this matter'. The phrase 'بر دل مکن' literally 'do not make (it) upon the heart' = 'do not let it enter your mind/heart.' 'مکن' is the negative imperative/jussive 'do not'. 'هیچ' functions adverbially here ('at all/ever').
segments.s176-g147: The first 'که' introduces a content clause; 'بیگمانم' is a first-person present-form meaning 'I am certain' (not merely an adverb), and the second 'که' introduces the clause whose content the speaker is certain of. Word order places the speaker's stance ('من بیگمانم') before the content clause.
segments.s176-g148: 'بیاید' is a 3rd-person present/subjunctive used here as an expectation/future 'will come'. The place complement 'دمان' (hem/skirt/edge) follows the verb in poetic word order. 'تا' expresses extent 'as far as/up to' and governs 'لب رود آب' = 'the lip/brink of the river' (i.e., the riverbank).
segments.s176-g149: Again the object 'مرا' appears before the verb and is marked by 'را'. 'برکشد' (here) carries the sense of lifting/putting up—so together with 'زنده بر دار' it means 'will lift/hang (me) alive upon the gallows.' 'خوار' after 'دار' qualifies the result as 'despised/held in contempt.' The verb form is 3rd-person singular future/expectation.
segments.s176-g150: 'فرنگیس را' is a marked object (Farangis). 'با تو' literally 'with you' here functions idiomatically as 'in your presence' or 'before you'; 'ای' is the vocative particle attached to 'تو' to address the king directly: 'O you, O king.' The phrase is final for poetic effect.
segments.s176-g151: 'به آب افگند' = 'threw/cast into the water' with 'به' indicating direction/into. The clause 'ماهیان‌تان خورند' places the possessor-suffixed noun 'ماهیان‌تان' ('your fishes') before the verb 'خورند' ('they eat' / 'will eat'). The verb is 3rd-person plural and expresses the action/result.
segments.s176-g152: 'وگر' = 'but if' / 'if otherwise' introduces the alternative. 'زیر نعل' = 'under the horseshoe' (i.e., under the hoof). 'اندرون' reinforces 'inside/underneath.' 'بسپرند' (present/subjunctive 3rd pl.) is from سپردن 'to consign/commit' and here means 'they will be consigned/left under the hoof' — i.e., trampled/committed to that fate.
segments.s176-g17: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'). خاک is the noun 'earth/soil' and functions as the subject of the predicate that follows. بودیش is an older/poetic form combining بود ('was') with the enclitic -یش ('his/its'), so here it means 'was his' or 'belonged to him'. پروردگار is the predicate noun 'lord/nurturer' placed after the clause to yield 'if the earth were his lord.'
segments.s176-g18: ندیدی is the negative past form (here used in a conditional-result sense: 'would not see / did not see'). دو چشم من is the direct object phrase 'my two eyes' (literal 'two eyes of me'), and این روزگار is a temporal phrase 'these times / this age' placed at the end to indicate what is not seen. Together the clause gives the conditional result: 'my two eyes would not see this time.'
segments.s176-g19: سپهرم here is a proper name (Sepahram). بدو is the dative/prepositional phrase 'to him' (به + او in older form). گفت is the past verb 'said' (here presented as the speech verb). کاسان is an interrogative/indefinite 'those people / who are those', and بدی functions adjectivally as 'bad/evil' modifying کاسان ('those evil ones'). The segment marks the speech introduction: 'Sepahram said to him, "Who are those evil ones..."'.
segments.s176-g20: اگر begins a conditional clause ('if'). دل is 'heart' (courage, inner feeling) and is the semantic subject. ز is the poetic preposition 'from / because of'. لشکر is 'army/host'. هراسان is an adjective 'frightened', modifying دل (i.e., 'heart frightened by the army'). بدی again appears as a predicative/adjectival notion ('bad / cowardly' in this context), so the whole clause reads 'if your heart is frightened by the army'. This completes the conditional started before.
segments.s176-g21: یکی is an indefinite numeral 'one / a certain'. گیو گودرز names the man (Giv, of Goudarz); in the epic names may be juxtaposed like this. بودست is an archaic past copula meaning 'there was' (بود + است fused in poetic usage). و بس is a short clause 'and enough / and that was sufficient' (بس = sufficient). The line therefore states 'there was one (Giv son of Goudarz), and that was enough.'
segments.s176-g22: سوار is 'rider/horseman' and is the noun being negated. ایچ is the emphatic negative quantifier 'nothing / none'. با او is the prepositional phrase 'with him'. ندیدند is the past plural 'they did not see' (here used in an impersonal/collective sense). کس here functions as the negative indefinite 'no one' (the subject that did not see). Word order is emphatic/poetic: 'No one saw any rider with him.'
segments.s176-g23: ستوه آمد is a fixed expression 'became weary / grew tired' with آمد serving as the light verb. از is the preposition 'from / out of'. چنگ literally 'claw' but here 'grip' or 'hold', a figurative object of the weariness. یک تن سپاه is a quantified genitive 'one person of the army' — 'a single soldier of the host.' The clause means 'he became weary of the grip of a single soldier of the army.'
segments.s176-g24: همی is an older/perfective-aspect marker that here gives an ongoing or habitual sense ('was going / kept going'). رفت is the past of رفتن 'went'. Then follows a coordinated list of proper names: گیو و فرنگیس و شاه = 'Giv and Farangis and the king.' Together: 'Giv and Farangis and the king went on/left.'
segments.s176-g193: چنان (so/thus) + خوار (humiliated/despised) form a manner phrase modifying the verb برگشت (returned). برگشت is a past tense verb 'turned back/returned'. ز (an archaic poetic form of 'از') means 'from', and رودبان ('boatman') is the agent; word order is adverb–adjective–verb–prepositional phrase (typical poetic inversion).
segments.s176-g194: که introduces the result/consequence clause ('so ... that ...'). جان را uses جان as a vocative/object 'life/soul' with را as the definite/object marker. همی گفت is the archaic narrative perfect/iterative sense 'kept saying' (همی marking continuous or habitual past in poetry). پدرودمان is an interjection/phrase meaning 'our/final farewell' (i.e. 'farewell'); together the clause reports the boatman's repeated farewell.
segments.s176-g195: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آمد is the past tense 'came'. به + نزدیکی + باژگاه is a prepositional phrase 'to/near the toll‑house' (به + noun of nearness + the place noun). The whole segment means 'when he came near the toll‑house.'
segments.s176-g196: هم آنگه is a temporal/attendant phrase meaning 'also/at once' or 'straightaway then' (هم reinforcing immediacy). ز + توران marks origin 'from Turan'. بیامد سپاه is a verb‑subject word order common in poetry: بیامد (came) + سپاه (the army) — 'an army came.'
segments.s176-g197: چو 'when' introduces the temporal clause. نزدیک رود آمد places the adverbial نزدیک 'near' before the noun رود 'river' and the verb آمد 'came'; the name افراسیاب follows as the subject (poetic word order: verb + subject at the end). The segment reads 'When Afrasiab came near the river.'
segments.s176-g198: ندید is the past negative 'did not see'. ایچ مردم is an emphatic negative phrase 'not a single person' (ایچ = 'nothing/none'). کشتی برآب is a compound 'ship(s) on the water' (برآب = 'on the water'); the clause lists two negatives ('no people, no ship') describing what he saw.
segments.s176-g199: یکی is the indefinite 'someone/a certain one'. بانگ زد is an idiom 'cried out/shouted' (زد here functions with بانگ to mean 'uttered a cry'); زد takes the sense 'to sound/perform' in this collocation. تند is an adverb/adjective 'sharp/fierce/quickly' modifying how the cry was made. بر + باژخواه marks the target 'at/to the toll‑collector' (بر used as 'toward/at' in such contexts).
segments.s176-g200: که introduces reported speech/content ('that...'). چون یافت is a temporal/conditional clause 'when/as soon as he found' (چون = 'as/when' + یافت past 'found'). این دیو بر آب راه is a noun phrase 'this demon on the water‑way' (بر آب راه = 'on the water path/river route'), the object of یافت; the clause reports what was found.
segments.s176-g153: بدو is a prepositional/adverbial phrase meaning 'to him' placed before the verb (word-order: indirect object before verb). گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (here used with KIḴUSROW as the speaker). کیخسرو is the proper name/subject; اینست و بس is a nominal clause 'this is enough' where اینست = 'this is' and بس = 'enough' (بس is emphatic/absolute here).
segments.s176-g154: پناهم is a nominal phrase 'my refuge' (پناه + possessive -م). به Yezdān is a prepositional phrase 'in/to God' marking the locus or source of refuge. فریادرس is an appositive or predicate noun 'the rescuer/succorer' that describes Yezdān; the whole phrase means 'my refuge is in God, the rescuer.'
segments.s176-g155: فرود آمد is a two-part past tense meaning 'he alighted / dismounted' (فرود = descent, آمد = came). از is the preposition 'from'. باره here has an archaic sense like 'burden / pack / saddle' (an element associated with travel) and راه‌جوی is an appositive 'the way-seeker / traveler' identifying who dismounted. The subject is implicit (he).
segments.s176-g156: بمالید is a past verb 'he rubbed' (context implies rubbing his face). و links the two verbs. بنهاد is an archaic past 'he placed / laid (down)' (here 'laid his face down'). بر governs the following NP and means 'upon / on'. خاک = 'earth, ground' and روی = 'face' (object of the verbs). The object ('his face') is expressed by روی and is shared by both verbs.
segments.s176-g157: همی is an adverb meaning 'continually / kept on' (marks iterative or ongoing action). گفت here again functions as 'said' (past). پشت و پناهم lists two nouns 'support/back and my refuge' (پناهم contains the possessive -م). توی here functions as a locative/predicative element meaning roughly 'in you' or 'you are (it)' — i.e. the addressee is being identified as the speaker's support/refuge.
segments.s176-g158: نماینده is a noun 'representative / embodiment' used predicatively of the addressee. رای here means 'counsel / plan' and is linked to نماینده to mean 'embodiment of counsel'. و راهم 'and my way' (راهم = راه + possessive -م) continues the list of what the addressee is for the speaker. توی again functions locatively/predicatively ('in you' / 'you are'). The clause as a whole predicates these roles of the addressee.
segments.s176-g159: درستی و پستی lists 'uprightness/virtue and baseness/meanness'. مرا (split in the tokenization as م + را) marks the speaker as possessed/affected ('my' / 'me' as the thing concerned). فر is the noun 'fr' = 'royal glory / splendour' used predicatively. تست here is an archaic present form ('thou art' / 'you are') linking the addressee as the predicate ('you are the splendour of my virtue and baseness').
segments.s176-g160: روان و خرد 'soul/spirit and wisdom' are the domains being described. سایه پر is a compound-like expression: سایه = 'shade / shelter' and پر (postpositive) = 'full (of)' so سایه‌پر = 'full shelter' or 'full of shade/protection'. تست again is the archaic 'you are', yielding 'you are the full shelter over my soul and my wisdom.'
segments.s176-g33: The clause literally reads 'to him he saw on (the) saddle bound like a stone.' The object pronoun ورا appears before the verb (a common poetic order), دید is simple past 'saw,' and بر زین ببسته is a participial/compound adjectival phrase (ببسته = 'bound/tied') describing the person as 'tied on the saddle.' چو + سنگ is a simile 'like a stone.'
segments.s176-g34: This noun phrase continues the description: 'two hands from behind (the) back with (a) palahang.' دو دست is the noun phrase 'two hands'; از پسِ پشت ('از' + پس + پشت) expresses location 'from/behind the back' (literally 'from after the back'); با introduces accompaniment or instrument 'with,' here attaching پالهنگ (a leopard/leopard-skin) to the scene (likely 'with a leopard-skin/cloak'). The phrase modifies the bound person seen in the previous segment.
segments.s176-g35: بپرسید is past tense 'he asked' (poetic past form). The conjunction و links clauses; زو (ز + ـو) means 'from him' or 'because of him' and is the source of the feeling. ماند اندر شگفت is a verbless/result clause 'remained in wonder' (ماند = 'remained/was left,' اندر شگفت = 'in astonishment'), so the whole means he asked and was left amazed.
segments.s176-g36: غمی گشت is an intransitive idiom 'sorrow arose / there came grief' (گشت = 'became/arose'), with و linking the next clause. اندیشه اندر گرفت is a causative-like phrasing 'thought seized (him)' — اندیشه 'thought' is the subject that گـرفت (took/seized) inwardly (اندر = 'within/inside'), i.e. 'thought took hold.'
segments.s176-g37: بدو گفت پیران is inverted word order common in Persian poetry: بدو ('to him') + گفت ('said') + پیران ('the elders') — literally 'to him said the elders.' که introduces the reported clause ('that ...'). شیر ژیان is a compound epithet: شیر 'lion' with ژیان (a poetic qualifier), here functioning as the head of the reported clause.
segments.s176-g38: This is a correlative negation: نه ... و نه ... = 'neither ... nor ...'. درنده is an adjectival qualifier 'predatory/ravenous' modifying گرگ ('wolf'), و links the two negatives, and ببر is 'tiger.' بیان appears at the end as a nominal referring to 'expression/description' or 'to be spoken of' — here the clause denies that the lion should be thought of as a ravenous wolf or tiger.
segments.s176-g39: نباشد is an archaic/poetic negative copular/existential form meaning 'there is not' or 'may there not be' (here declarative: 'there is not'). چنان 'such' qualifies what is denied, and در صف کارزار is a locative phrase 'in the battle line/formation' — together: 'such does not occur in the battle line.'
segments.s176-g40: کجا here is rhetorical ('how/where') rather than a simple interrogative; گیو تنها = 'Giv alone' (Giv is the proper name), and بد‌ای شهریار is an exclamatory/vocative phrase addressing the king (بد‌ای is an archaic interjection 'ill/alas' or 'woe'; شهریار 'O king'). The whole asks rhetorically, 'How could Giv be alone, O king?' linking back to the claim that such a thing does not happen in battle.
segments.s176-g161: به آب اندرون = a prepositional phrase meaning 'into/in the water'; دلفزایم is a verbal/adjectival form meaning 'it delights me' or 'you delight my heart' with the enclitic -م ('me'). توی here is the poetic/emphatic form of 'تو' (you) placed after the predicate for emphasis. The line therefore literally says 'in the inner water you delight me.'
segments.s176-g162: به خشکی = 'on/to the dry land'; همان رهنمایم = 'the same (is) my guide' where همان ('the same/that very') points back to the preceding clause and رهنمایم (رهنما + -یم) means 'my guide.' توی again is the emphatic/poetic 'you' after the predicate. The clause parallels the first: 'on dry land you are likewise my guide.'
segments.s176-g163: به آب اندر + افگند = 'threw/cast into the water' (اندر = in/into). افگند is a past (perfective) verb 'threw' or 'cast' (third person); خسرو سیاه follows as the direct object, with سیاه as an epithet after the name ('Khosrow the dark/black'). Word order is verb plus object inverted for poetic effect.
segments.s176-g164: چو = 'as/like'; کشتی همی راند = 'as (if) a ship he kept steering/sailing' where همی is the frequentative/continuous particle and راند is the verb 'drove/steered' (poetic present/habitual). تا باژگاه = 'until/to the toll‑house' with تا marking the extent/destination.
segments.s176-g165: پس = 'then/thereupon.' او is the third‑person pronoun 'he/that one' used here in the line's economy of expression; the following names فرنگیس و گیو دلیر are presented in apposition, with دلیر ('brave') modifying گیو. In context the phrase identifies the persons meant before the comment about fear.
segments.s176-g166: نترسد is a negative present/poetic perfect form meaning 'did not fear' (classical narrative often uses such forms to narrate past action). ز = 'from/because of' (poetic), جیحون is the name of the river (Amu Darya), زان = 'from that/and that', and آب شیر literally 'water of the lion' (here an epithet for fierce/turbulent water). Together: 'they did not fear the Jeyhun and that lion‑water.'
segments.s176-g167: بدان سو is an archaic/prepositional phrase meaning 'toward that side' (ب + دان + سو fused for poetic economy). گذشتند is past plural 'they passed/passed on.' هر سه درست literally 'each/ all three proper/right' = 'the three (of them) rightly' or 'all three were upright/they three passed rightly.' The adjective درست follows the numeral, a common poetic order.
segments.s176-g168: جهانجوی is an epithet 'world‑seeking/ambitious' placed before the name it qualifies; خسرو is the proper name. سر و تن بشست = 'washed his head and body' where بشست is the past tense 'he washed' and سر و تن ('head and body') are the direct objects linked by و. The clause states a subsequent action by Khosrow.
segments.s176-g81: میانشان = میان + enclitic -شان, placed before the verb phrase to mean “between them.” ببرم is a 1st-person singular subjunctive/precative form (classical/poetic) meaning “I will cut / let me cut” (from بریدن); Persian often uses this form to state the speaker’s intended action. به + شمشیر تیز is an instrumental prepositional phrase where به means “by/with” and the adjective تیز follows the noun (noun + adjective order).
segments.s176-g82: به ماهی دهم contains the preposition به marking beneficiary/recipient (“to/for the fish”) and the verb دهم (1sg present/subjunctive of دادن) meaning “I give / I will give.” تا introduces a purposive/result clause “so that / in order that,” کند is 3rd person singular present subjunctive of کردن used causatively (“may/might make” or “lets it cause”), and the reduplicated ریز ریز intensifies smallness — “in little bits / bit by bit.”
segments.s176-g83: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning “when.” کیخسرو is the proper name (subject). ایران is the object/goal. بجوید is 3rd-person singular present (classical) of جستن/جویدن, “seeks/searches.” همی is an archaic continuous particle placed after the verb to mark ongoing or habitual action (“is/ever”).
segments.s176-g84: فرنگیس is the personal name (subject of the question). باری is an adverbial/discourse particle here meaning roughly “then/now/this time” (an older usage); چه is the interrogative “what.” پوید is 3rd-person singular present of پویدن, here “pursues / seeks”; همی again is the continuous particle marking ongoing action.
segments.s176-g85: خود is an emphatic/reflexive pronoun meaning “himself” or “he himself.” و is the coordinating conjunction “and.” سرکشان here refers to his chiefs/men (an archaic/poetic plural formation). سوی is the preposition “toward,” and جیحون (Jeyhun) is the place-name (Amu Darya). کشید is a past tense (classical) of کشیدن meaning “drew / moved / hurried” (here “moved/drew toward”), used to describe the motion.
segments.s176-g86: همی again is the old continuous particle prefacing the following action. دامن is “garment/hem/mantle” (object). از is the preposition “from.” چشم is “eye” (here part of the phrase ‘from the eyes’). در خون is an adverbial phrase “in/with blood” (در = “in/into,” خون = “blood”). کشید is the same past form of کشیدن, here describing the action (‘drew’/‘pulled’/‘caused’), yielding the sense “he drew his garment and blood came from his eyes.” The exact poetic word order is somewhat compressed/elliptical.
segments.s176-g87: به هومان بفرمود = به + name marks the indirect object “to Hooman,” and بفرمود is an honorific/past verb meaning “he said/commanded.” کاندر is an archaic fusion of که + اندر (“that/in”), introducing the content of the order; شتاب is the noun “haste.” Together they yield “he told Hooman that (in) haste ….”
segments.s176-g88: عنان را بکش is an imperative clause: عنان “reins” is the object, را is the definite/direct-object marker, and بکش is the imperative of کشیدن meaning “pull/draw!” (here a command to shorten the reins). تا introduces a limiting clause “until”; لب here means “brink/edge,” and رود آب (poetic ordering) refers to the water (rūd/river) — the sense is “until the water reaches the brim/edge.” The whole clause is an imperative with a temporal limit.
segments.s176-g1: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. آگه شد is a two-word predicate 'became aware' (آگه = aware, شد = became) and takes از + noun to mark what he became aware of; here از لشگر = 'of the army'. The subject افراسیاب is postposed (poetic inversion) after the predicate 'آگه شد'.
segments.s176-g2: برو here is the contracted/prepositional sense 'بر او' = 'over/on him' (poetic contraction of two words) introducing who is affected. تیره شد is simple past 'became dark'. تابش آفتاب is a genitive noun phrase 'the sun's radiance' (تابش = radiance; آفتاب = sun) and is the thing that 'became dark'.
segments.s176-g3: بزد is past-tense 'he struck/beat'. The coordinated objects کوس و نای ('drum and reed-flute') follow it, then a second coordinated verb phrase و سپه برنشاند: سپه is the direct object of برنشاند ('he set forth/arrayed the army'). The subject is omitted (continuing from the previous line) and the two verbs are linked by و.
segments.s176-g4: ز ایوان marks source/place ('from the iwan/portico') with poetic ز for 'from'. به کردار آتش is a simile phrase 'in the manner/likeness of fire' (به = 'in/like', کردار = 'manner/deed', آتش = 'fire'). براند is simple past 'drove' (he drove/send onward). Together: 'he drove them from the portico as if fire.'
segments.s176-g5: دو منزل یکی کرد is an idiomatic past construction 'he made two stages one' = he covered two stages as one (i.e., hurried over distance); کرد is the past 'made/caused'. و آمد دوان follows: آمد = 'came' and دوان ('running') modifies manner (came running).
segments.s176-g6: همی is an epic/progressive particle indicating continuity ('kept/continued to'); تاخت is past 'galloped/rushed'. برسان تیر literally places the verb before its object: برسان = 'sent/caused to reach' and تیر = 'arrows'; از کمان marks source 'from the bow' (از = from, کمان = bow). Overall: 'he rushed on, sending arrows from his bow.'
segments.s176-g7: بیاورد is past 'he brought'. لشکر is the direct object 'the army'. بران is a deictic/adverbial 'to that/thereon' (pointing to the battlefield), and رزمگاه is the destination 'battlefield'. Word order places object then deictic then location.
segments.s176-g8: که introduces a subordinate/relative clause 'for/which' explaining or specifying; آورد is past 'brought' with کلباد بد as its object (a name plus epithet: 'Kalbad the wicked'). با سپاه is an adjunct 'with an army' (با = with, سپاه = army). The implied subject of آورد is the same actor as earlier (he).
segments.s176-g105: همی‌گر is a conditional particle here ('if / were to'); گذر is the noun 'passage / crossing' and بایدت is the modal 'you must' with the enclitic ـت marking the second person ('باید + ـت' = 'you must'). ز introduces the source ('from') and آب رود together describe the running water or river ('آب' + جنبهٔ توصیفی 'رود' = river/running water). The clause literally reads 'If (همی‌گر) a crossing (گذر) is necessary for you (بایدت) from the river water (ز آب رود)'.
segments.s176-g106: فرستاد here is a bare verb stem meaning 'to send / dispatch' used with the modal باید (word order V + باید = 'it is necessary to/send must'); به marks the direction/recipient ('to') and کشتی is the indirect object 'the ship'; درود is the noun 'greeting / salute' serving as the thing to be sent. Together: 'one ought to send a greeting to the ship.'
segments.s176-g107: بدو is the prepositional clitic meaning 'to him' ('to that one') and precedes گفت (past 'said') in the poetic word order; گیو is the speaker's name. آنچ 'that which' + خواهی 'you want' form a free relative 'whatever you want', and بخواه is the 2nd‑person imperative ('ask' or 'wish'): 'Ask whatever you want.'
segments.s176-g108: گذر ده is an imperative phrase 'give passage / let (someone) pass' (noun + imperative ده). که introduces a subordinate clause giving the reason ('for / because'), and تنگ اندر آمد سپاه is inverted poetic order: آمد is the past verb 'came', سپاه is its subject 'the army' placed finally, and تنگ اندر indicates 'into a narrow (place) / narrow pass'. So: 'let him pass, for the army has come into a narrow pass.'
segments.s176-g109: بخواهم is 1st‑person present/future 'I will ask / I want' and here begins reported speech; ز تو marks the source 'from you'; باج is the object 'tribute / toll'; گفت is the matrix verb 'he said' reporting the speech; اندکی is an adverb/adjective 'a little / small amount' modifying the tribute. The reported clause is embedded in the past narrative via گفت.
segments.s176-g110: ازین is از + این ('from this / of these'); چار is 'four'; چیزت is 'your things' (چیز + enclitic ـت 'your'), here meaning 'the four items you possess / four things'; بخواهم repeats the 1st‑person 'I will ask'; یکی is 'one' (i.e. one of them). The sequence says 'of these four things I will ask one.'
segments.s176-g111: زره خواهم از تو is 'I will ask (خواهم) a coat of mail / armor (زره) from you (از تو)'. The following گر functions as an adversative/alternative particle ('or / if rather'), introducing the next option; اسپ سیاه is the noun + adjective order typical in poetry ('a black steed'). Grammatically the speaker enumerates alternatives after stating the first demand.
segments.s176-g112: پرستار here functions as one of the listed alternatives ('a keeper / attendant' or similar), and وگر ('and/or if') introduces the next alternative. پور is the noun 'son (of)' used before a parent's name, and فرخنده ماه is a compound/name (literally 'Farrokhandeh‑Māh' / 'fortunate moon') serving as the parent: پور فرخنده ماه = 'the son of Farrokhandeh‑Māh'. The whole segment lists further possible things demanded.
segments.s176-g113: بدو گفت is a two-word clause meaning “he said to him”: بدو (to him) is a prepositional/indirect-object phrase placed before the verb گفت (said). گیو‌ای is a vocative — Giv with the vocative particle ای — addressing the man directly. گسسته خرد is a descriptive adjective phrase (literally “broken-wisdom” = ‘of broken mind’ or ‘mad, shattered in reason’) postposed to the name as a characteristic.
segments.s176-g114: سخن is the noun “speech/words” (the thing to be spoken). زان = ز + آن (“from that” / “from it”) links the forthcoming content to a previously mentioned sign. نشان is “sign/mark”. گوی here functions as the verbal/imperative/gerund notion “say / speaking” (the listed noun sense “saying” is the closest label); کاندر = «که اندر» (contracted) = “in which / wherein”. خورد in this context carries the idiomatic sense “befall / fall into / suit” (i.e. “which falls into/which is fitting”), so the clause means “speak the words from that sign which suit/fall within it.”
segments.s176-g115: به هر باژ‌گر is a prepositional phrase ‘to every toll-collector’ (به = to/for, هر = every). شاه is vocative or appositional here (“O king” / “the king”); شهری is used as ‘a town/city (to grant)’ and بدی in the surface form is ambiguous in older Persian — here the overall line is best read as a transferred-action statement “(the) king gave a town to each toll-collector.” Because the lexical list does not include a plain verbal sense for بدی in this line, that item is semantically irregular/archaic in form.
segments.s176-g116: ترا is the object or dative phrasing (‘to you’ / “you” with clitic t + را); زین جهان literally ‘from this world’ (زین = of/from this; جهان = world). نیز = also. بهری (from بهر) here means ‘benefit/advantage’ (the object or result). بدی repeats the same ambiguous form as above; taken with the rest it yields ‘you also took/received advantage from this world’ (the line uses older/formal diction where the inflection of ‘give/take’ may appear unusual).
segments.s176-g117: The first که introduces a purpose/subjunctive clause (‘that / so that’). باشی is the subjunctive/potential 2nd-person form ‘may you be’. The following که introduces a relative clause ‘who/that’; شه را کنی = ‘make the king’ (شه = king, را = object marker, کنی = subjunctive/potential ‘you make/do’). خواستار is a predicative/adjectival noun ‘desirous/one who desires’ — together the clause reads ‘that you may be one who desires (to) make the king.’
segments.s176-g118: چنین = ‘thus / such’. باد here is the optative particle ‘may’ (classical Persian uses باد + subjunctive to express a wish). پیمایی is a subjunctive/infinitival form from پیمودن ‘to traverse / to accomplish’ and with باد gives ‘may (your) traversing/journey be thus’ or ‘may you make such a journey’. ای is the vocative ‘O’, and بادسار is the addressee ‘wind-bearer / wind-master’ (a personified epithet).
segments.s176-g119: وگر = ‘if / but if’. مادر شاه = ‘the mother of the king’ (مادر = mother, شاه = king). خواهی is the 2nd-person present/future/optative of خواستن ‘to want/desire’ = ‘you want/wish’. همی is an enclitic emphasizing continuity or immediacy (‘indeed / still’). The clause thus means ‘and if you desire the king’s mother (i.e. aim at that high station)...’
segments.s176-g120: به introduces the following phrase (here instrumental/associative: ‘with / for’). باژ = ‘cry/noise’ (an expressive word often marking an exultant shout). افسر ماه = ‘the moon’s helmet/crown’ (افسر = helmet/crown, ماه = moon; a poetic image). خواهی همی repeats the verb ‘you will/would desire’ with the emphatic همی. The whole second clause reads roughly ‘you will indeed desire (even) the moon’s crown’ — an image for aspiring to the highest glory.
segments.s176-g73: یکی بانگ برزد is a compact past clause: یکی 'someone' is an indefinite subject; بانگ برزد is a past action 'uttered a cry' where برزد is an archaic/periphrastic verb used with بانگ. ز پیشش is a prepositional phrase 'from/before him' (پیشش = in his presence), and براند is the (archaic/narrative) third‑person verb راندن 'to drive (away)' — here it follows the cry and indicates driving away (object omitted). Word order follows older Persian: Verb can appear before/after its adverbial phrase and the subject is minimal.
segments.s176-g74: بپیچید پیران is past narrative 'the elders turned/twisted' (بپیچید = they turned away); پیران is the plural subject 'elders'. و خامش بماند links a second result: خامش 'silent' (adjective/noun) + بماند 'remained' (past/resultative), literally 'and remained silent.' Note the verb بماند is used to state the resulting state.
segments.s176-g75: ازان پس is a temporal adverbial 'after that/thereupon'. به مغز اندر is the directional phrase 'into the brain' (به ... اندر = into). افگند is the past verb 'cast/threw' and باد here is the noun 'wind' (the thing cast) — together the clause says literally 'thereupon a wind cast into the brain,' i.e. a stirring entered his mind.
segments.s176-g76: به دشنام و سوگند is an instrumental/phrasing 'with insults and oaths' (به marking manner). لب برگشاد is a fixed phrase 'opened his lips' (لب = lip/mouth; برگشاد is past 'opened'); together it means 'he opened his lips to utter curses and oaths.' The verb برگشاد governs the object (لب) and the manner is given by the prepositional phrase before it.
segments.s176-g77: که‌گر is the conditional 'if'. گیو و کیخسرو are the coordinated subjects (proper names). دیوزاد is a predicate/adjective 'demon‑born' modifying them. The clause sets up a conditional proposition 'if X are demon‑born.'
segments.s176-g78: شوند is the plural subjunctive/optative of شدن 'become' used in a wished/conditional sense. The rest is a verbless nominal phrase describing what they (or the consequence) should be: ابر 'cloud' qualified by غرنده‌گر 'roaring' and تیز 'swift/sharp', and باد 'wind' as the noun predicate — overall a curse/wish: 'let the cloud be a roaring, swift wind.' Word order is poetic: predicate noun and adjectives follow the verb شوند.
segments.s176-g79: فرود آورمشان is the compound verb 'فرود آوردن' meaning 'to bring down'; آورمشان is first‑person singular with the object suffix -شان 'them' ('I will bring them down'). ز ابر بلند is the source phrase 'from the high/lofty cloud.' The clause therefore expresses a first‑person threat or intention to bring them down.
segments.s176-g80: بزد دست is literally 'I/ he struck (with) [the] hand' (بزد is a past/performative verb 'struck'); contextually the subject remains the speaker ('I') and the phrase indicates striking with the hand. و ز گرز بگشاد بند continues the action: ز before گرز is instrumental 'with the mace', بگشاد is 'opened/loosed' (past/perfective form used resultatively), and بند is 'fetter/bond' — together 'and with the mace loosened the fetter.' Poetic narration mixes tenses but lists sequential actions.
